sections.heading: Support for physical punishment rose nationally
sections.body: In 2025, 18.3 percent of mothers and caretakers believed that a child needs to be physically punished, up from 11.9 percent in 2022. This page measures attitudes, not direct child-disciplining behavior, so the change should be read as a shift in stated belief. Even with that caution, the 6.4-point increase is the central national pattern in the data.

sections.heading: CAR and Northern Mindanao were the highest regions
sections.body: Cordillera Administrative Region (CAR) had the highest 2025 level, with 42.6 percent of mothers and caretakers saying physical punishment is needed. Northern Mindanao followed at 36.6 percent, while Davao Region was also elevated at 29.0 percent. These regions stand well above the national level of 18.3 percent.

sections.heading: The lowest regions were below or near 10 percent
sections.body: Central Visayas had the lowest 2025 level at 7.7 percent, followed by Ilocos Region at 8.0 percent and Cagayan Valley at 11.3 percent. The gap between CAR and Central Visayas was 34.9 percentage points. This regional spread is much wider than most subgroup differences outside the regional table.

sections.heading: Most regions increased from 2022 to 2025
sections.body: The national increase was reflected across much of the regional table. CAR rose from 16.7 percent in 2022 to 42.6 percent in 2025, Northern Mindanao from 19.9 to 36.6 percent, and Davao Region from 15.1 to 29.0 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ also increased, from 20.0 to 25.4 percent. These changes point to a broad rise in stated acceptance rather than a pattern confined to one area.

sections.heading: A few regions moved against the national increase
sections.body: Not every region increased. Central Visayas declined from 9.8 percent in 2022 to 7.7 percent in 2025, and Ilocos Region edged down from 8.6 to 8.0 percent. BARMM changed only slightly, from 13.7 to 15.1 percent. These exceptions show that the national rise did not erase low-attitude regions or produce a uniform regional pattern.

sections.heading: Male respondents were more likely to support physical punishment
sections.body: In 2025, 20.6 percent of male respondents believed physical punishment is needed, compared with 17.6 percent of female respondents. The change since 2022 was larger among males, rising from 10.9 percent, while females rose from 12.2 percent. The 2025 sex gap is modest, but it reverses the 2022 ordering.

sections.heading: Urban and rural levels were identical in 2025
sections.body: Urban and rural respondents both registered 18.3 percent in 2025. In 2022, the urban value was 12.6 percent and the rural value was 11.3 percent, so both residence groups increased by 2025. Unlike the regional table, residence does not divide the 2025 attitude profile.

sections.heading: The highest level was among respondents aged 25 to 34
sections.body: Respondents aged 25 to 34 had the highest 2025 level at 20.9 percent. The other age groups were lower and close to each other: 16.5 percent among those younger than 25, 18.0 percent among those aged 35 to 49, and 16.6 percent among those aged 50 or older. All age groups increased from 2022, with the largest rise among those aged 25 to 34.

sections.heading: Education did not show a simple gradient
sections.body: The highest 2025 value by mother's educational attainment was among the post-secondary group at 27.0 percent, followed by Grades 1 to 6 at 20.6 percent and college at 19.5 percent. Grades 7 to 10 stood at 17.4 percent, while the no-education group was 16.6 percent. The pattern does not support a simple conclusion that higher education is consistently associated with lower acceptance of physical punishment.

sections.heading: Wealth differences were present but uneven
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile had a 2025 level of 20.1 percent, while the highest quintile had 16.1 percent. The middle quintile was also relatively low at 16.2 percent, but the fourth quintile was 19.9 percent, close to the lowest quintile. This suggests some wealth-related contrast, but not a smooth step-by-step gradient across all quintiles.

takeaways: Nationally, belief that a child needs to be physically punished rose from 11.9 percent in 2022 to 18.3 percent in 2025.
Regional differences are large, with CAR at 42.6 percent and Central Visayas at 7.7 percent in 2025.
Residence does not separate the 2025 profile, while sex, age, education, and wealth show smaller and less uniform differences.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
